Just as a bit of background, Filspari is an oral, non-immunosuppressive therapy designed to reduce proteinuria, which is the excess protein leakage into urine. We typically see this as a key driver of kidney damage progression in FSGS. Mechanistically speaking, it acts as a dual endothelin angiotensin receptor antagonist, in that it can simultaneously block endothelin A and angiotensin II pathways. These are two very important systems we know contribute to things like kidney inflammation, scarring, and hemodynamic stress.

In terms of the newly approved indication, it’s specifically for adults and pediatric patients, so above 8 years old, with FSGS who do not have nephrotic syndrome. Just to be precise, this is a subgroup defined by lower levels of proteinuria and also the absence of severe systemic symptoms. Clinically, this makes the treatment options highly meaningful because treatment guidelines from KDIGO emphasize early proteinuria reduction even before we see full nephrotic syndrome develop. It then sets up Filspari as a disease-modifying option rather than just another symptomatic management drug.

So that full approval means Filspari now represents the first-ever FDA-approved therapy for FSGS. And yes, the condition is quite rare, but it’s also very serious, and its treatment has for a long time relied on off-label treatments like corticosteroids and general blood pressure drugs. You see, before this approval, Filspari had already been approved for IgA Nephropathy (IgAN), which is another rare kidney disease. For that use case, it works to slow kidney function decline. But the new FSGS approval is going to expand this role significantly, from a single-disease therapy into a broader rare kidney disease franchise. This made the upside case and was the reason a lot of bullish investors bought in, leading to the decision date.

Keep in mind that this distinction is important. For IgAN, the primary goal is slowing long-term kidney function decline, whereas in FSGS, especially for the non-nephrotic patients, the emphasis is more on reducing proteinuria as an early intervention to then delay progression to kidney failure. This was clinically supported by the Phase 3 DUPLEX study, going into the approval. It showed statistically significant and sustained reductions in proteinuria versus irbesartan, which is a standard-of-care angiotensin receptor blocker, which helped reinforce Filspari’s superior efficacy in this setting. What I find important here is that this approval aligns rather closely with modern treatment paradigms in that it targets early proteinuria reduction, which is central to KDIGO guidelines, and it does it by using a non-immunosuppressive, disease-modifying approach rather than just relying on late-stage or even steroid-based treatments.

I think, going into this decision date and especially towards the end of FY25, some investors were becoming cautious about Travere’s outlook. So this development definitely strengthens their commercial outlook for FY26. The full approval is also nice to see, as opposed to accelerated or conditional approval, and it should reduce regulatory uncertainty going forward. However, what we really want to see now is broader physician adoption and payer coverage. That's the key to this approval translating to higher earnings and longer-term revenue visibility.

A word on the TAM

I also see the overall picture of Filspari becoming a multi-indication asset. So that should increase its peak sales potential and lifecycle value. And the fact that it is the only approved therapy for FSGS so far does create a kind of first-mover advantage with limited direct competition. So, that being said, I would expect this new setup to be more supportive of premium pricing, especially in rare disease markets where unmet need is pretty high. It’s also worth mentioning that even though the expansion should accelerate uptake among nephrologists, investors still need to weigh in factors like safety monitoring requirements, such as the liver toxicity REMS program.

So the expanded TAM for Travere is definitely significant. They’re set to layer FSGS onto an already growing IgAN base. And based on company disclosures, the combined U.S. opportunity would now exceed about 100,000 patients, including about 30,000 in the newly approved non-nephrotic FSGS group. If we take the known U.S. list price in the range of $9,900/month or $118,800 annually, it gives us a theoretical incremental TAM from FSGS alone that starts to approach $3 to $4 billion annually at full penetration. However, we usually see that rare-disease drugs typically reach 20% to 40% penetration over time, so there’s a more realistic peak incremental revenue opportunity here of $0.7 to $1.5 billion. For context here, Filspari brought in about $322 million in FY25 sales in IgAN alone. So even partial uptake in FSGS could see a step-change of about 2x to 4x in revenue over several years, rather than marginal growth.

As for earnings impact and timing, the key point to remember is that revenue acceleration can begin almost immediately simply because the drug is already commercialized, with decent distribution and reimbursement pathways in place. We already have some physician familiarity, which means that it is more like a label expansion and not a new launch. However, the earnings inflection is expected to lag revenue somewhat and will likely become visible in 2027 to 2028, as operating leverage improves. There will inevitably be some fixed commercialization costs absorbed across a larger patient base here. I think we can expect pricing to very much remain consistent across indications since it’s pretty typical for rare disease markets where pricing is tied to mechanism and disease severity rather than indication-specific discounts. The real economic leader for me here is therefore volume expansion, not any price increases.

Valuation

For the updated valuation, Travere is now trading at a valuation that already reflects some very high-growth expectations. However, there is also some significant execution risk here, which is why their overall valuation grade might screen quite poorly relative to the sector. On a forward basis, the stock trades at about 26x 2026 earnings, a premium to the healthcare median, which sits around 18x, whilst the EV to sales multiple of 5.6x forward is also at a premium, at about 68% above peers. So investors are clearly pricing in some meaningful revenue expansion from Filspari. However, I see the most telling metric as the extreme 365x forward GAAP price to earnings, which would imply depressed near-term profitability and also goes to show that the story is still not about current earnings here, but more so future scaling. We have a sharp compression in projected price to earnings from about 46x in 2025 to about 9x by 2027. We can pair this with a triple-digit EPS growth forecast in the 190% range for 2027. So the expectation would be for rapid inflection from early commercial stage to earnings leverage, which would mainly be driven by Filspari ramping across indications.

Looking ahead

So just to sum up, the Filspari approval here for FSGS should help Travere move more towards a scalable rare-disease franchise and also help meaningfully expand their growth runway. The key from here going forward is execution, specifically physician uptake and payer coverage. We need this before we can see that expanded TAM convert into sustained revenue growth. And whilst the long-term story remains pretty compelling, near-term upside may depend on how quickly the market actually digests the recent rally and whether adoption trends can start to validate the bull case from this expanded TAM.

data.title: ClearBridge Global Infrastructure Income Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
By Daniel Chu, CFA, Charles Hamieh, Shane Hurst, and Nick Langley

Real Assets, Real Resilience

Market Overview

Listed infrastructure outperformed global equities in the first quarter of 2026 as a selloff in software stocks drove a market rotation into more defensive sectors and war in Iran raised inflation fears and underscored a flight to safety.

For the quarter, natural gas utilities and pipelines led the listed infrastructure sectors in our universe; renewables and North American and European utilities also delivered strong returns, helped by rising earnings expectations driven by higher capex needs to support increased power demand, generation mix changes, asset resiliency and modernization programs. GDP-sensitive toll roads, airports and rails also held onto gains; North American freight rails benefited from improved economic conditions, with the Purchasing Managers Index entering expansionary territory for the first time in three years. In Europe, we saw some M&A activity, notably from French electric utility Engie (ENGIY), which acquired UK Electric Networks. Several airport companies, meanwhile, began negotiations for airport charges for their next regulatory cycle; they are proposing to meaningfully step up investments to support traffic growth across their platforms.

The escalation of military tensions with Iran in March led to supply disruptions across key energy and commodity markets, including oil, LNG and other critical resources moving through the Middle East. This contributed to renewed inflation pressures globally as higher energy, transport and input costs flow through supply chains.

This commodity disruption played out in sector performance in March, with natural gas utilities and pipelines delivering strong returns, North American and European utilities modestly down but still outperforming the major infrastructure indexes and global equities, and GDP-sensitive user toll roads, airports and rails underperforming amid heightened concerns about inflation, consumer spending and overall economic activity.

Performance Overview

On a regional basis, the U.S. and Canada region was the top contributor for the quarter, with U.S. electric utility Entergy the lead performer. Entergy (ETR) provides services to approximately three million people in Arkansas, Texas, Mississippi and Louisiana; share prices rose with the announcement of another capex raise during its earnings release and on enthusiasm for its converting 8 GW of turbines into more data center deals.

Turning to Western Europe, U.K. electric utility SSE (SSEZY) also performed well. SSE is a diversified energy utility headquartered in Scotland and the U.K.’s largest renewable energy generator. It is vertically integrated, operating over the entire supply chain in the U.K., with generation (including hydro, wind, CCGT), electricity networks, and retail businesses (primarily B2B). SSE’s share price rose as funding risks diminished and concerns around the U.K. macroeconomic outlook eased.

French airport operator Aeroports de Paris (AEOXF) and U.S. electric utility Constellation Energy (CEG) were the largest detractors for the quarter.

Aeroports de Paris owns and operates three airports in Paris (Charles de Gaulle, Orly and Le Bourget), as well as minority stakes in several global airport groups, including TAV in Turkey and Schiphol in Holland. The company received an unfavorable outcome for its 2026 aeronautical tariff filing, which the market interpreted as a negative read-through for the 2027–2034 tariff period. While the decision is disappointing, follow-up discussions with management gave us confidence that a more balanced outcome will ultimately be achieved. This conviction is underpinned by the company’s extensive stakeholder engagement with the French government, airlines and its regulator, which has confirmed strong support for the proposed capex plan. The plan aims to modularly expand the Paris airport network, easing congestion and unlocking capacity to support future growth. A final regulatory decision is expected in the second half of 2026.

Constellation Energy is primarily a nuclear generation company and is the largest producer of carbon-free electricity in the U.S., serving states including New York, Illinois, Maryland, Pennsylvania and New Jersey. The company’s combined generation capacity is more than 32 GW and 90% of annual output is carbon free. Constellation’s share price was pressured by calls for emergency auctions for electricity at PJM Interconnection, the largest U.S. power grid, which signaled increased future capacity. We believe this reaction is overdone.

Portfolio Positioning

During the quarter, we initiated positions in Mexican airport operator Grupo Aeropuertos Del Sureste (ASR), where we expect traffic resilience despite a new competing airport terminal ramping in Cancun, and Canadian energy infrastructure company Pembina Pipeline (PBA), where we think AI-related projects will continue to drive EBITDA upside. We also bought French rail operator Getlink, whose new dividend policy represents a nearly 40% increase compared to last year, with a commitment to gradually increase it by 5 cents per share annually through 2030. We see scope for more enhancements to shareholder returns in coming years, given the company’s strong balance sheet and expected business performance across its key business units. We expect its core car and truck shuttle business to deliver above-inflation yield growth and low-single-digit traffic growth over the medium term, while its high-speed rail franchise should see a meaningful acceleration in traffic in the 2030s as new routes are developed into other countries such as Germany and Switzerland and other parts of France.

We exited four positions, taking profits after periods of outperformance in U.S. rail operator Union Pacific (UNP) and Canadian electric utility Emera (EMA). We sold out of Spanish electric utilities Redeia (RDEIF), following a downgrade in our growth expectations after recent regulatory outcomes, and Iberdrola (IBDSF), where we maintain a positive view, although we see more attractive investment propositions in other European utility companies, specifically Engie.

Outlook

In this environment, infrastructure assets continue to be resilient because many operate under regulatory frameworks or long-term contractual structures that allow inflation and cost increases to be passed through to end users over time. As a result, while higher interest rates and macro volatility may create short-term valuation pressure, the underlying cash flow characteristics of infrastructure assets provide inflation protection following periods of geopolitical-driven commodity shocks.

More broadly, we are observing greater appreciation by the market of real assets, given their inflation protection in the current macroeconomic and fiscal backdrop.

Across our holdings, we continue to expect low double-digit internal rates of return over the next five years, underpinned by defensive dividend profiles and growth outlooks. We believe we are still in the early stages of an accelerated infrastructure investment cycle, driven by expanding mobility needs, rising energy demand, a shifting energy mix and the modernization of networks to enhance resilience against physical asset and environmental risks. We believe these investments underpin durable growth in earnings and dividends for investors.

Portfolio Highlights

We believe an absolute return, inflation-linked benchmark is the most appropriate primary measure against which to evaluate the long-term performance of our infrastructure strategies. The approach ensures the focus of portfolio construction remains on delivering consistent absolute real returns over the long term.

On an absolute basis, the Strategy saw positive contributions from all nine sectors in which it was invested in the quarter (out of 10), with electric utilities, energy infrastructure and gas utilities the top contributors.

Relative to the FTSE Global Core Infrastructure 50/50 and on a U.S. dollar basis, the Strategy outperformed in the quarter, driven primarily by stock selection in the electric utility sector and a lack of communications holdings. Stock selection in the energy infrastructure sector and an overweight to other infrastructure detracted.

On an individual stock basis, the top contributors to absolute returns in the quarter were Entergy, SSE, Williams (WMB), South Bow (SOBO) and TC Energy (TRP). The main detractors were Constellation Energy, Aeroports de Paris and Atlas Arteria (MAQAF), while Pembina Pipeline and Emera also lagged.

Daniel Chu, CFA, Portfolio Manager

Charles Hamieh, Portfolio Manager

Shane Hurst, Portfolio Manager

Nick Langley, Head of Real Assets, Portfolio Manager

data.title: RQI: What Real Estate Should Be
data.description:
We all have an idea of what a real estate investment should look like. For most folks, this looks something like owning a cash flow producing asset that delivers rent checks. The combination of monthly rent and long term capital appreciation supported by factors like market rent growth and inflation is what makes real estate such an appealing investment.

Whether people are looking to buy a house to rent, apartments, an office building, the warehouse, or something else entirely, they quickly learn that a commercial asset is much more like running a business than a passive investment. The promise of passive income is quickly spoiled by the need to retain and vacant space, perform repairs and maintenance, another task to quickly eat into your time let alone bottom line. This leads people to the world of REITs.

Although individual REITs are diversified across hundreds or thousands of assets, there is still a clear degree of business risk. Perhaps the best example is a company like Alexandria Real Estate (ARE), which has seen massive price declines and dividend cuts despite still owning high quality assets.

The past five years have uncovered a volatile REIT environment where many fan favorites have run into trouble. It seems like REIT picking has become considerably more difficult. So, where do we go from here?

I propose you consider the Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) from Cohen & Steers (CNS).

Why RQI?

In the past, I have covered RQI from a variety of angles. While touching on different parts of the fund, my core thesis has remained that it is simply the closest thing we can get to what I imagine a real estate investment should be.

Zooming back just a couple of paragraphs, we all want something that includes high current income with long-term capital appreciation. This is just what RQI has been able to deliver.

RQI is structured as a closed end fund with a flexibly assembled portfolio of real estate companies with a smaller sleeve of debt and preferreds.

The fund also dabbles in listed and unlisted options as well as other derivatives associated with the fund’s borrowing. Along the lines of borrowing, the fund is leveraged with roughly one-third of total assets being borrowed.

Since RQI is a closed end fund, the manager uses a managed distribution policy. This means sending a level monthly dividend to shareholders of the fund. In the case of RQI, this distribution remained remarkably consistent over time. In 2016, RQI opted to switch to monthly distributions, setting a $0.08 per share per month payout. For the next decade, RQI sent shareholders the same monthly distribution, every month of every year. Occasionally, there was a special year-end distribution as well.

This year, the fund increased the monthly distribution to $0.09 per share per month, an increase of 12.5%. Again, this degree of consistency is uncommon even for closed end funds with a managed distribution policy.

The why behind RQI is relatively simple. We want dependable monthly income that amounts to a considerable long-term return. The first part is easy; the second part is considerably harder.

Performance

Performance for closed end funds is a tricky gambit. Essentially all closed end funds have high distribution yields because of the tax structure associated with a RIC. But current yield is not the best assessor of long-term return. These funds, like REITs themselves, are forced to distribute essentially all income and capital gains to shareholders or face penalties.

This means that over time, many funds that opt for a level distribution policy end up overdistributing. Closed end funds that hold debt typically see a more stable net asset value, while funds that hold equities see their net asset value become considerably more volatile. Over time, this volatility can cause net asset value erosion if the manager chooses to maintain a level distribution policy. NAV erosion is a tricky and often misused term.

NAV erosion occurs when a fund overdistributes to shareholders consistently over time. This means a piece of the fund’s net asset value beyond earnings is going back to shareholders to pay them their distribution. Do this enough times and the small amount of NAV being sent to shareholders begins to compound. This overdistribution in real estate closed end funds is best

In the world of real estate funds, this overdistribution is best illustrated in funds like the CBRE Global Real Estate Income Fund (IGR) or the abrdn Global Premier Properties Fund (AWP), both of which pay double-digit distribution yields. Over the past ten years, both funds have seen their net asset values decline considerably.

While their distributions have remained relatively stable or even increased, net asset value has declined to offset total return.

RQI does something different. The fund’s distribution yield is considerably lower than either competing fund at 8.8%. Since 2013, the fund's net asset value has grown by around 20%.

Over the long term, this has led RQI to outperform competing funds, despite paying a lower distribution.

With net asset value remaining more consistent over time, it leads me to view RQI as a superior investment compared to either of the two competing funds.

Under The Hood

Something you’ll notice quickly is that the 8.4% distribution is still considerably higher than the REIT market more broadly and the fund’s top holdings. Right now, the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) pays a TTM yield of 3.73%, less than half of the distribution yield of RQI.

RQI is an actively managed fund, which means the managers are constantly buying and selling stocks, bonds, and options of companies within their investment sphere. This creates ongoing capital gains and losses, which are taken into consideration to align with the fund’s distribution. RQI collects dividends from REIT holdings, interest from bonds, and premiums from options, which are included in the distribution. Supplementing to close the gap are these capital gains reaped from buying and selling REIT stock within the portfolio.

Following a record year for some companies in the portfolio, it appears as though RQI is reaping some serious gains.

As it stands, the first six distributions of 2026 have all been funded by long term capital gains, according to CEF Connect. For taxable investors, this is a considerable benefit built into the distribution that boosts taxable equivalent yield even further.

Some might be asking where the gains are coming from. There are actually some pockets of real estate that have been quite strong. For example, Welltower (WELL) remains the largest holding at 16.3% of assets.

WELL has seen a generational boost over the past three years. Senior housing has seen extraordinary demand shifts in the past few years, and owner-operators of all shapes and sizes are reaping some extraordinary benefits. WELL is the largest senior living landlord and currently trades at a white knuckle valuation of 40X trailing FFO. Rising over 50% in the past year, WELL has some big shoes to fill in terms of anticipated growth with the current dividend yield sitting at just 1.4%.

Beyond senior living, AI continues to propel demand for data centers and infrastructure REITs as secondary beneficiaries. Looking in the top ten holdings of RQI, some of the largest names like Digital Realty Trust (DLR) and American Tower Corporation (AMT) account for another 15% of assets.

Most important is that the portfolio is constantly changing. Several years ago, industrial packed the top holdings of the portfolio as the demand wave following the pandemic boosted industrial REITs. Today, industrial holdings have trimmed down to just Prologis (PLD) in the top ten holdings at 7%. While management is moving the needle, market shifts alone are responsible for a large piece of the portfolio’s change. In the case of WELL, RQI owned the same number of shares from Q3 to Q4, but the concentration increased considerably because WELL shares appreciated by 30%.

When you look under the hood of RQI, you realize that the investment is seriously diversified. The fund has over 200 individual holdings, each of which stems from a diversified operation. This means exposure to a truly global pool of assets managed by an equally wide pool of asset managers.

Conclusion

For a hands-off real estate opportunity, it really doesn’t get much better than RQI. The fund has a stable net asset value and a distribution, which has led to an attractive total return profile over time.

Even better, RQI continues to be an outperformer, besting VNQ over most time horizons, including the past decade.

With a small distribution bump going into this year, the proof is in the pudding that RQI is cut from another cloth when it comes to real estate closed end funds. Stacked up against funds like AWP and IGR, RQI looks like a superior opportunity when considering net asset value stability. RQI continues to earn a Buy rating, deserving to be on your short list of real estate closed end funds.
